issue_title: start gdb with --nx command line option (do not read any .gdbinit files in any directory)
issue_body:
At work we use a company wide gdbinit file that provides pretty-printers for some common types (both in-house types and c++ std types) - it seems to be buggy, so in some cases it causes gdb to hang when responding to `1-stack-list-variables --simple-values`, which causes gdbgui to hang in turn.

I suggest to start gdb with `--nx` either always or conditioned on some command line option (e.g. `--nx` or `--safe-mode`). I hard-coded it at my end into `statemanager.py` when creating `GdbController` and it does the trick ...

issue_title: Crash "pew wipeenv venv" like "pew rm venv"
issue_body:
Please consider a bash script in "strict mode" (*)

```
set -euo pipefail
IFS=$'\n\t'

VENV_NAME=venv

pew wipeenv $VENV_NAME
pew rm      $VENV_NAME
pew new -d  $VENV_NAME
pew in      $VENV_NAME pip install --upgrade blahblah
```

If the venv does not exist the script will show
`
ERROR: Environment 'venv' does not exist.
`
and exit.

If we comment out the line with wipeenv and run the script (with not existing venv), the script will NOT crash at rm, the result will be:
`
Error while trying to remove the d:\Users\myuser\.local\share\virtualenvs\venv env:
The system cannot find the path specified
`
but the bash script will continue. 

I think that the reason is that wipenv returns a different error code from rm or otherwise crashes in less pleasant way.

I suggest that wipeenv crashes in the same way as rm, for consistency.

I checked the above on Windows 10 with Git's GNU bash, version 3.1.23(6), no access to Linux today but probably it would work in the same way.

(*)
http://redsymbol.net/articles/unofficial-bash-strict-mode/

So I have a structure at the moment that looks like this 

```
import curio
class Base:
    async def loop(self):
        pass
    async def run(self):
        while True:
            await self.loop()

class Something(Base):
    async def loop(self):
        await curio.sleep(1)
        print("looping in %s" % await curio.current_task())

s = Something()
curio.run(s.run())
```

Now because it was Base.run that was scheduled as a task in `run` it will report `Base.run` every time. This is less helpful when debugging in the monitor as it shows and I don't know which of those Bases is which task. 

```
0      READ_WAIT    337        None    Kernel._run.<locals>._kernel_task                 
1      QUEUE_GET    1          None    Monitor.monitor_task                              
2      QUEUE_GET    1          None    Base.run                                     
3      FUTURE_WAIT  1          None    UniversalQueue._get_helper                        
4      QUEUE_GET    96717      0.00036 Base.run                                     
5      TASK_JOIN    37277      None    Base.run                                     
6      QUEUE_GET    46194      0.05448 Base.run    
```

Is there a way that I could hint to curio what to name a task, or provide some attribute or API for renaming a task to something useful after scheduling.

As profile provider names are now gender-specific, it would be great if the class accepted a parameter to allow the user to specify the gender of the profile, e.g profile = fake.profile(sex='M').

Is this within scope? I did a rudimentary test addition to providers/profile/__init__.py, (python 3) -  there was no issue.

Hi Heng, 

Just came across a bizarre issue which I have been debugging all day to try and get at the root cause. 

To cut a long story short, it looks like in some circumstances the SAM header gets spread out in chunks throughout the SAM file - almost like there are multiple SAM files concatenated together.

I only see this when aligning to a reference that is effectively a database of fasta files concatenated together. 

For example: 

```
minimap2 -a DB.fasta my.fastq > my.sam
``` 

I have a file `DB.fasta` which has 16498 entries in it. When I align my fastq file to this reference the resulting SAM file has 4 separate header locations spread throughout the file. Each section is unique. 

To check my sanity I ran the exact same thing with `bwa mem` and there are no issues.   
Also, if I just align the sample to a single reference (with `minimap2`) such as GRCh38, the resulting SAM is fine.

When trying to view the resulting SAM file in `samtools view` I get an error saying the file is truncated. Therefore I was only able to see this in `vim`.

My current install of `minimap2` is `2.0-r191-dirty`

Let me know if you want some more information.

From the README:

> `git_push` – adds `--set-upstream origin $branch` to previous failed `git push`;

This isn't actually the case, `--set-upstream` isn't added to the previous push, the entire command is replaced with just `git push --set-upstream origin $branch` regardless of whether there were any args.

Example:

```
$ git push --quiet
fatal: The current branch new-branch has no upstream branch.
To push the current branch and set the remote as upstream, use

    git push --set-upstream origin new-branch

$ fuck
git push --set-upstream origin new-branch [enter/↑/↓/ctrl+c]
```

The `--quiet` arg is lost, ideally `git push --set-upstream origin new-branch --quiet` would be suggested.

issue_title: Grammar not properly checked for enumeration, bits and identityref built-in types
issue_body:
Based on code review (not actually running pyang) the following code fragments will not be detected as errors, even though they are erroneous:

```
leaf foo {type enumeration;}
leaf bar {type bits;}
leaf meh {type identityref;}
```

The first two are due to new code that deals with new 1.1 restrictions. The check is implemented in `types`, yet you never call it.

statements.py

``` python
    elif enums != []: # this check for empty should be removed
        stmt.i_is_derived = True
        enum_spec = types.validate_enums(ctx.errors, enums, stmt)
```

types.py

``` python
def validate_enums(errors, enums, stmt):
    if enums == []:
        err_add(errors, stmt.pos, 'MISSING_TYPE_SPEC', # should only report this if stmt.arg is 'enumeration'
('enumeration', 'enum'))
```

Similar applies to bits.

You never implemented such a check for idbases. There needs to be at least one 'base' present if type is built-in 'identityref'.

Jernej

[enumbitbase@2016-10-19.yang.txt](https://github.com/mbj4668/pyang/files/538909/enumbitbase.2016-10-19.yang.txt)

```python
--> 157         ax.add_artist(legend)
    158 
    159     if plot_testdata:

/usr/lib64/python3.4/site-packages/matplotlib/axes/_base.py in add_artist(self, a)

-> 1649         a.axes = self
```

issue_title: Results of passive scan not adding to report or alerts
issue_body:
Zap-cli has been showing some odd behavior when I to view the results thus far of a passive scan. I set zap-cli to passive scan on url 127.0.0.1 and port 8080, and then had firefox proxy through this but when I run zap-cli zap-cli alerts -l Low -f json it returns empty brackets. When I run zap-cli report -o report.xml it returns a file like this: 

``<?xml version="1.0"?><OWASPZAPReport version="2.7.0" generated="Wed, 6 Jun 2018 17:59:43">
<site name="http://example.com" host="example.com" port="80" ssl="false"><alerts></alerts></site><site name="http://ocsp.digicert.com" host="ocsp.digicert.com" port="80" ssl="false"><alerts></alerts></site><site name="http://pagead2.googlesyndication.com" host="pagead2.googlesyndication.com" port="80" ssl="false"><alerts></alerts></site><site name="http://platform.twitter.com" host="platform.twitter.com" port="80" ssl="false"><alerts></alerts></site><site name="http://m.addthisedge.com" host="m.addthisedge.com" port="80" ssl="false"><alerts></alerts></site><site name="http://m.addthis.com" host="m.addthis.com" port="80" ssl="false"><alerts></alerts></site><site name="http://randomcolour.com" host="randomcolour.com" port="80" ssl="false"><alerts></alerts></site><site name="http://www.theuselessweb.com" host="www.theuselessweb.com" port="80" ssl="false"><alerts></alerts></site><site name="http://s7.addthis.com" host="s7.addthis.com" port="80" ssl="false"><alerts></alerts></site><site name="http://www.ismycomputeron.com" host="www.ismycomputeron.com" port="80" ssl="false"><alerts></alerts></site><site name="http://redscientist.com" host="redscientist.com" port="80" ssl="false"><alerts></alerts></site></OWASPZAPReport>`` 

All the websites I've been visiting are listed, but no vulnerabilities.

What's even more odd is that I've tried troubleshooting this and I've gotten the correct output from alerts but only after a significant amount of time. In one case I used the alerts and reports function (but nothing else) a few times for a half an hour and they weren't returning what I wanted them to. I then left my computer for another half an hour and came back and then ran the alerts command I mentioned earlier. It gave the passive scan results I wanted on all the websites I had visited in the hour I had it open the first time I ran it. The data for the vulnerabilities is definitely there but something seems to be holding it up, and I'm not sure what it is or how I'm eventually getting the report I want other than waiting long enough. Active scan and quick scan results are added to the report immediately, but passive scan results seem to be held up by something or sometimes not display at all.

keyring imports the gnomekeyring module, which uses gtk2. 

This breaks any Gtk3 applications in which it is loaded.

I'm not sure what the best solution is to work around this - it seems pretty hard to determine if the current application is a gtk2 or gtk3 app, especially if keyring is imported before Gtk. It would be nice if there was at least some way of disabling the GNOME backend for keyring, so applications that are aware of this issue can work around it.

issue_title: keyring leaks password into process table on OS X
issue_body:
Based on casual reading of the code in keyring/backends/OS_X.py, this code shells out to security(1), passing it the '-w password' option.  This leaks the password into the process table.

Unfortunately, security(1) does not provide any alternative methods of supplying the password, but it's possible to use a hack around it by creating a fifo, writing the password into the fifo and then using the shell's redirection mechanism to get input from the fifo to the command.

I found a performance bottleneck in the IAPWS97 module.
Here my analysis (in Jupyter notebook):

For whole function on my machine it take (tested in Region 2):
```python
%timeit iapws97.IAPWS97(P=3., s=6.5)
```
100 loops, best of 3: 9.36 ms per loop

```python
import iapws97
from scipy.optimize import newton

P, s = 3., 6.5
# initial value of Temperature
To = iapws97._Backward2a_T_Ps(P, s)
funcion = lambda T: iapws97._Region2(T, P)["s"]-s
# fsolve
a = %timeit -o -q iapws97.fsolve(funcion, To)[0]
val = iapws97.fsolve(funcion, To)[0]
print("{:10s} {:.4f} {}".format('fsolve', a.best, val))
# newton
a = %timeit -o -q newton(funcion, To)
val = newton(funcion, To)
print("{:10s} {:.4f} {}".format('newton', a.best, val))
```
fsolve       0.0080 564.036985544
newton     0.0005 564.036985544

The bottleneck is in 'fsolve' method which performs 85% of time for whole function. If we change 'fsolve' method to 'newton' we get significant speed improvement.

```python
%timeit iapws97.IAPWS97(P=3., s=6.5)
```
1000 loops, best of 3: 1.36 ms per loop

The [newton](http://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.optimize.newton.html#scipy.optimize.newton) method is much simpler than [fsolve](http://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.optimize.fsolve.html#scipy.optimize.fsolve) but for given tasks it enough.

issue_title: remove documentation about speciality of setupterm's descriptor, use os.devnull
issue_body:
It's actually perfectly fine to call ``curses.setupterm(kind, open(os.devnull).fileno())``.

More importantly, this removes a chunk of documentation and comments, such as:
```
            Terminal initialization sequences will be sent to ``stream`` if it
            has a file descriptor and to ``sys.__stdout__`` otherwise. (``setupterm()`` demands to send them
            somewhere, and stdout is probably where the output is ultimately
            headed. If not, stderr is probably bound to the same terminal.)
```

and

```
            # (...) Explicit args make setupterm()
            # work even when -s is passed to nosetests. Lean toward sending
            # init sequences to the stream if it has a file descriptor, and
            # send them to stdout as a fallback, since they have to go
            # somewhere.
```

This issue backports upstream https://github.com/erikrose/blessings/issues/95

issue_title: Account for partially-covered leaves in log analyzer
issue_body: As of now, the behavior is to calculate the leaf fluence as one pixel, which does not allow for partially-covered leaves. This may (slightly) affect the gamma score. One solution is to calculate the fluence more realistically in the y-direction. The pixels would simply be mapped to the realistic leaf width.

issue_title: decode_compressed_response=True changes the original response header
issue_body:
If you use `decode_compressed_response=True`, you actually add the `vcr.filter.decode_reponse` as response filter.

Inside this method, you could find this:
```python
    headers = CaseInsensitiveDict(response['headers'])
    if is_compressed(headers):
        response = copy.deepcopy(response)
        encoding = headers['content-encoding'][0]
        headers['content-encoding'].remove(encoding)
...
        response['headers'] = dict(headers)
```

The objective is to do a deepcopy of response of avoid to change it. However, it's changing the `headers` anyway.

Because the "headers" values are list and `CaseInsensitiveDict` does not do a copy of the values. Since the "remove(encoding)" is applied on the "headers" dict AFTER the deepcopy but on the "headers" created BEFORE, you change the actual headers list:

```python
In [1]: from vcr.util import CaseInsensitiveDict
In [2]: response_header = {"content-encoding":['gzip']}
In [3]: headers = CaseInsensitiveDict(response_header)
In [4]: import copy
In [5]: new_response_header = copy.deepcopy(response_header)
In [6]: headers['content-encoding'].remove('gzip')
In [7]: response_header
Out[7]: {'content-encoding': []}
```

Recreating the CaseInsentiveDict after the deepcopy solves the problem.

issue_title: Messages should not be printed to stdout when used as a library
issue_body:
It'd be nice if the library printed messages/errors via the standard Python `logging` module. Right now, the library prints to `stdout` in all circumstances, which bypasses logging functionality (and goes against the [common practice of libraries not printing to `stdout`](http://docs.python-guide.org/en/latest/writing/logging/)).

As you already use the `_log_action` method for all print messages, it would be straightforward to change the `print(...)` statement within it to use instead the logging system, and enable a stream handler pointed at `stdout` for the command line interface.

It would be nice if `NetNS` support was better isolated from the underlying nuts and bolts, and more in line with the rest of `pyroute2`/`IPRoute` design:

1. Namespace creation "in exclusive mode" requires import of `os` for `O_CREAT` and `O_EXCL` (by the way, there is an error in the documentation: it mentions `O_EXIST` flag. There is no such flag, `O_EXCL` must be used). In my opinion, it should use an own kwarg for that.
2. On attempt to create (exclusively) existing / remove non-existent namespace, `OSError` is raised. It should better be transposed into a `NetlinkError`.
3. `link('set',...)` accepts `Int` or `String` (netns name) for `net_ns_fd` kwarg. It would be logical if it could _also_ accept a ref to a `NetNS` object.

And somewhat related: it should be documented how does one move an interface out of a namespace back into the "default" namespace. I understand that one needs `link('set, ..., net_ns_fd=0)`, correct?

issue_title: UnboundLocalError thrown for certain timeout values
issue_body:
Folks - quick question about some strange behavior we are seeing on the consumer side.

We made the following change, in particular dropping out `session_time_ms`:
```bash
@@ -44,7 +44,7 @@ def get_kafka_consumer(topic,group_id):
                              api_version=KAFKA_API_VERSION,
                              auto_offset_reset='latest',
                              enable_auto_commit=False,
-                             session_timeout_ms=300000)
+                             session_timeout_ms=5000)
     return consumer
```

Which resulted in our code throwing the following error:
```bash
Exception in thread Thread-3:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib64/python2.7/threading.py", line 810, in __bootstrap_inner
    self.run()
  File "/usr/lib64/python2.7/threading.py", line 763, in run
    self.__target(*self.__args, **self.__kwargs)
  File "/usr/lib64/python2.7/multiprocessing/pool.py", line 380, in _handle_results
    task = get()
  File "<...>/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/kafka/protocol/pickle.py", line 32, in _unpickle_method
    return func.__get__(obj, cls)
UnboundLocalError: <unprintable UnboundLocalError object>
```

Looking at the code that throws the error:
```python
def _unpickle_method(func_name, obj, cls):
    for cls in cls.mro():
        try:
            func = cls.__dict__[func_name]
        except KeyError:
            pass
        else:
            break
        return func.__get__(obj, cls)
````

I assume that a `KeyError` causes execution to fall through without binding a value for `func`. Why does a timeout, or setting a time value too low, cause an `UnboundLocalError`?

I would like to help fix, but not sure what the cause is.

-Oscar

```
Command "python setup.py egg_info" failed with error code 1 in /tmp/pefile-2016.3.4
```

The issue here is circular dependency:
- `pefile` module depends on `future`
- `pip install` should install `future` automatically
- however `setup.py` imports version from `pefile` module before pip is able to install `future` module
If not, would it make sense to add such a feature?

The `.mpd` (MPEG-DASH) format is an xml document containing data about the media file. In the above example the video and audio can easily be extracted (the numbers 9 and 6 may vary):

audio: https://v.redd.it/24bqk13lf5fz/audio

issue_title: Need a robust connection & connection pool.
issue_body:
happybase currently use Thrift transport's `isOpen()` method (which is in fact the `TSocket` `isOpen()` method)  to determine [whether it should open a new connection or keep the current one](https://github.com/wbolster/happybase/blob/9cbd718c10a3089f234f1eac1236b631e1f8e7cd/happybase/connection.py#L164-L173). 

However, `isOpen()` only tells us whether you have closed this socket explicitly. (See [here](https://github.com/apache/thrift/blob/1fa273be5089070532908f159a0ccc38d7f7150c/lib/py/src/transport/TSocket.py#L44-L47) and [here](https://github.com/apache/thrift/blob/1fa273be5089070532908f159a0ccc38d7f7150c/lib/py/src/transport/TSocket.py#L72-L73) in thrift).

So, we should not use `isOpen` to determine whether we need a new connection. but to test the real socket object to see if the it is "dead" and should be closed.

Relative PR in thrift: https://github.com/apache/thrift/pull/945

issue_body:
At first I thought this was an error on all API attempts, maybe related to recent changes to Soundcloud from them adding the "Soundcloud Go" product.

I was trying to do:

```
~/projects/SoundScrape/soundscrape$ ./soundscrape.py -f https://soundcloud.com/zhu/in-the-morning
Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)
```

which is coming from the attempted JSON decoding of an HTTP 401:

```
(Pdb) requests.get(streams_url).json()
*** JSONDecodeError: Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)
```

the 401 shows up from:

```
(Pdb) requests.get(streams_url)
<Response [401]>
```

However, a little reading at Soundcloud forums turns up this:
[https://www.soundcloudcommunity.com/soundcloud/topics/soundcloud-go-api-access](https://www.soundcloudcommunity.com/soundcloud/topics/soundcloud-go-api-access)
which says:
>Thanks for writing on our help forum. I've done some research in that area and it is actually up to the uploading artist / distributor whether their content is fully available via the API.

issue_title: Remove (most) mock objects from whitelist files
issue_body:
We currently mock all objects in our whitelist files, e.g. `whitelist_defaultdict = Whitelist(); whitelist_defaultdict.default_factory`. This has the drawback that we can't test whether `default_factory` really exists (and is not misspelled or missing from the stdlib in this Python version). For non-dynamic objects, we should therefore access the objects directly instead of their mocks. The only place where we have to keep mock objects is the whitelist for the `ast` module, since these methods are created dynamically. To allow importing `collections` in the `collections.py` whitelist file, we have to rename it to `collections_whitelist.py` to avoid name clashes. Here is a diff that shows the necessary changes for `collections.py`:

```
diff --git a/vulture/core.py b/vulture/core.py
index 5400ae6..4f43e80 100644
--- a/vulture/core.py
+++ b/vulture/core.py
@@ -218,7 +218,7 @@ class Vulture(ast.NodeVisitor):
 
         unique_imports = set(item.name for item in self.defined_imports)
         for import_name in unique_imports:
-            path = os.path.join('whitelists', import_name) + '.py'
+            path = os.path.join('whitelists', import_name) + '_whitelist.py'
             if exclude_file(path):
                 self._log('Excluded whitelist:', path)
             else:
diff --git a/vulture/whitelists/collections_whitelist.py b/vulture/whitelists/collections_whitelist.py
index 4af0cdd..c43bc58 100644
--- a/vulture/whitelists/collections_whitelist.py
+++ b/vulture/whitelists/collections_whitelist.py
@@ -1,5 +1,4 @@
-from whitelist_utils import Whitelist
+import collections
 
 # To free memory, the "default_factory" attribute can be set to None.
-whitelist_defaultdict = Whitelist()
-whitelist_defaultdict.default_factory
+collections.defaultdict.default_factory
```

issue_title: Add a way to screen out importlib noise
issue_body:
I often use pyinstrument to profile importing a module to figure out ways to speed up imports. On python2 this works very nice but on python3 pyinstrument will pick up frames from importlib, making it much harder to visually see what's happening in the pyinstrument profile. This is happening because importlib was rewritten from C to python in python3, so pyinstrument is able to capture stack frames from it in python3.

To make that concrete, here's the profile for "import sympy" generated under python2:

https://gist.github.com/ngoldbaum/3e2f5213b45a64f67167d0dbd5878ad5

and python3:

https://gist.github.com/ngoldbaum/fb46b92f22a65181aa7442e2af2f51c1

issue_title: Version svn path doesn't support spaces
issue_body:
Version 0.20

Specifying a url or "trunk" as the version attribute of djangorecipe generates an svn command on line 251 of recipe.py:

cmd = 'svn co %s %s' % (svn_url, download_location)

If the project name / path contains any spaces, these do not get quoted or escaped, so the svn command sees the extra path parts as invalid command options. The fix is to quote the parameters, changing the line to:

cmd = 'svn co "%s" "%s"' % (svn_url, download_location)

hey @santosjorge - thoughts on the best way to plot multiple chart types, i.e. a `bar` and a `line`?

`type` as an `array` or a `dict`, sort of like how `secondary_y` works:
```
df = pd.DataFrame({
     'y1': [3, 1, 5], 
     'y2': [30, 50, 20]
}, index=[1, 2, 3])

df.iplot(kind={'y1': 'bar', 'y2': 'scatter'})

df.iplot(kind=['bar', 'scatter'])
```

this pattern could apply to any of the non-array figure properties, e.g.

```
df.iplot(mode=['markers', 'text'], marker=[{'color': 'blue}, {'color': 'red'}])
```

I think grouping could still be a lot easier. Here is an example with the Plotly syntax:

```
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv('http://www.stat.ubc.ca/~jenny/notOcto/STAT545A/examples/gapminder/data/gapminderDataFiveYear.txt', sep='\t')


py.iplot([
            {
                    'x': df[df['year']==year]['gdpPercap'],
                    'y': df[df['year']==year]['lifeExp'],
                    'name': year, 'mode': 'markers',
            } for year in [1952, 1982, 2007]
], filename='cufflinks/scatter-group-by')
```

with a `groupBy` and `groupVals` key that segments by traces, this could be a on-liner with:

```
df.iplot(x='gdpPercap', y='lifeExp', groupBy='year', groupVals=[1952, 1982, 2007])
```

where `groupVals` is `pd.unique(df[groupBy])` by default.

This could be extended to subplots really nicely too, where you could swap `groupBy` with `rows` and/or `cols` to split the traces out into multiple subplots.

issue_title: Outgoing edges of nodes should be sorted based on when a call `.stream()` created them
issue_body:
```python
i = ffmpeg.input("f1.mp4")
ref = ffmpeg.input("f2.mp4")
# BTW `filter_multi_output` is not in __all__, needs #65
s2ref = ffmpeg.filter_([i, ref], "scale2ref").node
scaled = s2ref[0]
ref2 = s2ref[1]
```
https://ffmpeg.org/ffmpeg-all.html#scale2ref

[![Screenshot_from_2018-01-26_14-33-23.png](https://s10.postimg.org/k87rpqofd/Screenshot_from_2018-01-26_14-33-23.png)](https://postimg.org/image/f9k9b7kmd/)

You would expect the output of `scale2ref` labeled 0 (`scaled`) to be the first output of `scale2ref` (because `.stream()` was called first), and 1 (`ref2`) to be the second. However, this doesn't always happen: it's totally up to `topo_sort()`.

I'll try to fix it and send a PR.

Inside the `end` method of `RevisionContextManager` 
```python 
    def end(self):
        """Ends a revision for this thread."""
        self._assert_active()
        self._stack.pop()
        if not self._stack:
            try:
                if not self.is_invalid():
                    # Save the revision data.
                    for manager, manager_context in self._objects.iteritems():
                        manager.save_revision(
```

I have some comprehension issue related to marshalling and parsing.

### Marshalling

Let's start with the marshalling. It is defined in the doc that it should be used `"to control what data you actually render in your response or expect as in input payload"`. So it is for *input* and *output* then? Why is the section called `"Response Marshalling"` if it also takes care of the input?

So on my end points, I do things as follow:
```py
@namespace.expect(user)
@namespace.marshal_with(user_full)
def put(self):
```

I have a different schema expected for input and marshalled for output.


### Parsing

Now here comes the thing. I defined what I want on entry, and output. What do I need Parsing for?

If my user schema looks like:
```py
user = namespace.model('User', {
    'id': fields.Integer(required=True),
    'first_name': fields.String(required=True),
    'last_name': fields.String(required=True),
    'email': fields.String(required=True),
    'age': fields.Integer(required=True)
})
```

Why do I need to define the parsing as follow, while the model is already defined?

```py
parser = reqparse.RequestParser()
parser.add_argument('id', required=True)
parser.add_argument('first_name', required=True)
parser.add_argument('last_name', required=True)
parser.add_argument('email', required=True)
parser.add_argument('age', required=True, type=int)
args = parser.parse_args()
```

Couple of things seems weird to me here:
- It looks *extremely* verbose to me. What is the point to expect a model if you have to parse it again?
- Why do I have to redefine the `type`, the `required`, everything actually?
- Why not simply: `args = parser.get_expected_model()`? (just an example: one line, instead of rewriting the whole model)

There must be something I don't understand, could someone clarify this for me?

issue_title: BUG in computeGaussianPerplexity when K > N - 1
issue_body:
In line [tsne.cpp#L37](https://github.com/DmitryUlyanov/Multicore-TSNE/blob/master/multicore_tsne/tsne.cpp#L374) vectors `indices` and `distances` will have `< K + 1` elements if `K > N - 1`.   
This will cause erroneous reads out of the bounds of the vector in [tsne.cpp#L388](https://github.com/DmitryUlyanov/Multicore-TSNE/blob/master/multicore_tsne/tsne.cpp#L388) and other loops.

There should be a check that `K <= N -1`.

issue_body:
Stack trace:
```
INFO:django.request:websocket.receive: Unknown error must be str, not bytes
Other Exception: must be str, not bytes
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File ".../lib/python3.6/site-packages/ws4redis/wsgi_server.py", line 123, in __call__
    recvmsg = RedisMessage(websocket.receive())
  File ".../lib/python3.6/site-packages/ws4redis/websocket.py", line 224, in receive
    raise e
  File ".../lib/python3.6/site-packages/ws4redis/websocket.py", line 215, in receive
    return self.read_message()
  File ".../lib/python3.6/site-packages/ws4redis/websocket.py", line 195, in read_message
    message += payload
TypeError: must be str, not bytes
```

Cause is the message initialization:
```python
message = ""
```
from: [here](https://github.com/jrief/django-websocket-redis/blob/e6634c2dfacd2cd2c6099d14a4a3194ab4bf02a2/ws4redis/websocket.py#L165).

The message is then concatenated with the websocket payload.

This works fine in Python 2 because the following is acceptable:
```python
"string" + b"bytes"
```

But of course, in Python 3 we get:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: Can't convert 'bytes' object to str implicitly
```

PR incoming.

issue_title: Add ModelField that accepts model instances instead of PKs
issue_body:
I often find myself wanting to accept model instances as inputs to my services. It would be nice if we could have a custom field that can accept model instances directly, instead of PKs when using  `ModelChoiceField`.

This would fix a few issues I've encountered when using `ModelChoiceField`:

### Prevent unintended model mixups

```python
person = Person.objects.get(pk=1)
tenant = Tenant.objects.get(pk=1)

SendInvoice.execute({'person': tenant.pk})
# meant to pass in person.pk, but because it's just an integer, it still validates
```

If we pass in model instances, we can check for its type.

```python
person = Person.objects.get(pk=1)
tenant = Tenant.objects.get(pk=1)

SendInvoice.execute({'person': tenant})
# raises InvalidInputsError, person is not an instance of Person
```

### Allow unsaved model instances as parameters

You could (optionally) pass unsaved model instances, as long as they're valid.

```python
person = Person(
    name='Mitch',
    age=24,
)

SendInvoice.execute({'person', person'})
# works

person = Person(
    name=1234,
)

SendInvoice.execute({'person', person'})
# raises InvalidInputsError (name should be string, age is required)
```

### Prevent multiple calls to the database

I find that even if `ModelChoiceField` only requires a PK, I'd still have to get the object first. This means that two database queries are made to fetch the same data.

```python
person = Person.objects.get(name='Mitch')  # fetch object from the database

SendInvoice.execute({'person': person.pk})  # the form would have to fetch the data from the database again
```

## Implementation

I feel like it would be relatively straightforward to implement a custom field that accepts a model instance as the parameter.

How I imagine the API to be:

```python
class SendInvoice(Service):
    person = ModelField('people.Person', allow_unsaved=True)
```

If someone could help with this, that'd be great.

Hi there. I'm trying to use pydal with an existing set of tables on my database and I'm hitting a wall here about how to deal with two rows of the same table appearing in the same query.

First a working example. Imagine I have two tables representing a directed graph: 

```python
from pydal import * 

db = DAL('mysql+mysqldb://someuser:somepassword@somehost:3306/graph')

db.define_table(
    'node',
    Field('name', type="string"),
    rname="someschema.some_rname_for_node"   
)

db.define_table(
    'edge',
    Field('edge_id', type="id"),
    Field('node_from', type=db.node),
    Field('node_to', type=db.node),
    rname="someschema.some_rname_for_edges"
)
```

Than I want the simplest query, just to print the names of the nodes that are connected by an edge:
```python
db.node.with_alias('start')
db.node.with_alias('end')
db.edge.with_alias('hop')

hop_join = (db.start.id == db.hop.node_from) & (db.end.id == db.hop.node_to)
print db(hop_join)._select(db.start.name, db.end.name)
```
The obtained query is:

```sql
SELECT 
    start.name, end.name
FROM
    graph.some_rname_for_node AS start,
    graph.some_rname_for_node AS end,
    graph.some_rname_for_edges AS hop
WHERE
    ((start.id = hop.node_from)
        AND (end.id = hop.node_to));
```
Which is exactly what I want. This works perfectly.

But for some reason, when I try this with my real database this doesn't work at all:
```python
db.define_table(
    'position',
    Field('position_id', rname='cargo_id', type='id'),
    Field('title', rname="titulo", type="string"),
    rname='cargo'
)

db.define_table(
    'similar_positions',
    Field('similar_id', rname='cargo_similar_id', type='id'),
    Field('position_from', rname='cargo_id1', type=db.position),
    Field('position_to', rname='cargo_id2', type=db.position),
    rname='cargo_similar'
)

db.position.with_alias("position_from")
db.position.with_alias("position_to")
db.similar_positions.with_alias("hop")

hop = (
    (db.position_from.id == db.hop.position_from) & 
    (db.position_to.id == db.hop.position_to)
)

print db(hop)._select(db.position_from.title, db.position_to.title)
```

The sql generated is:
```sql
SELECT 
    position_from.titulo, position_to.titulo
FROM
    cargo,
    cargo_similar AS hop,
    cargo AS position_from,
    cargo AS position_to
WHERE
    ((cargo.cargo_id = hop.cargo_id1)
        AND (cargo.cargo_id = hop.cargo_id2));
```
which fails to use the alias and ends up not producing the inner join I want (it actually returns all pairs of `position_from` and `position_to` regardless of whether there is a `hop` connecting them or not.

I can't spot the difference between the two codes except for the names of the tables and fields. What am I doing wrong?

Thanks for your time.

The` _common_util.append_response_error_content` function currently has an unused `**kwargs` argument as part of the function signature and a `#pylint: disable=unused-argument` comment to force pylint to ignore this.  In this case, pylint is correct in flagging `**kwargs` as unused and it should be removed along with the pylint "disable" comment.  Also the function could stand to be cleaned up and rewritten.  Suggest this:

```python
def append_response_error_content(response):
    resp_dict = response.json()
    if response.status_code >= 400 and resp_dict is not None:
        reason = resp_dict.get('reason', '')
        error = resp_dict.get('error', '')
        response.reason = '{0} {1}'.format(error, reason)
    return response
```

issue_title: Add ability to decode an object from a dict/json string based on provided class
issue_body:
Would be nice to have some functionality that allows object recreation based on provided class. For example:

```
class Thing(object):
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

obj = Thing('Awesome')
import jsonpickle
oneway = jsonpickle.encode(obj, unpicklable=False)
result = jsonpickle.decode(oneway, Thing) # or Thing.__class__
assert obj.name == result.name
```

issue_title: Checking if task is complete takes a very long time
issue_body:
I'm sure it has something to do with my usage, but dependency checking while kicking off my luigi job is taking forever (minutes, up to an hour even!).

Basic task dependency tree looks like this:

    [ Wrapper Task ]
    |
    `--[ Task A ] (can have multiple instances of this)
        |
        `--[ Task 1 ]
            |
            `--[ Task i ]
                |
                `--[ Task a ] (this "spawns" a bunch of tasks [read: 10000], looks like a massive scatter)
                    |
                    |--[ Task a1.0 ] (there are tons of these, which have a sibling)
                    `--[ Task a1.1 ] (this is the sibling, both of these sibling tasks are dependent on the same sub-task:)
                        |
                        `--[Task a1 child]
                            |
                            `--[ Task ! ] (Every single task spawned by Task a is dependent on this task, looks like a massive gather in a graph)
                                |
                                `-- [ Task 0 ] (this is the very first task which should do real work, basically creating the area to gather data to)

Is this so slow because it's checking the dependencies down through the entire chain at each level of tasks?  Is there a way to short-circuit dependency checking or is there a better method to organizing these tasks that I'm not seeing?

When i run the per_task_retry_policy example, the tasks are being retried indefinitely (or as configured in the luigi.cfg file). Effectively there is no per-task retry policy which affects the execution.

issue_title: Do not compute loss curves and maps from the asset loss table
issue_body:
After the change in https://github.com/gem/oq-engine/issues/3758,  loss curves and maps can be computed directly without the need of an EbrPostCalculator. It means that we can avoid saving the
asset loss table as intermediate step.

issue_title: SQS SendMessage does not use QueueUrl when endpoint is set
issue_body: As discussed with aws-sdk-php, current behavior is different and "wrong": https://github.com/aws/aws-sdk-php/issues/1009

issue_title: Created CloudFront signed cookies with boto3
issue_body:
I can't find a way to create signed CloudFront cookies in boto3. This is a really confusing part of restricting access to S3 buckets, but is necessary for my application. 

Is this currently possible?

issue_title: Doc rendering bug for dynamodb CreateTable
issue_body:
Compare our docs (http://boto3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/reference/services/dynamodb.html#DynamoDB.Client.create_table):

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/368057/10589850/73d5928c-7662-11e5-91c2-fd41b1c14f52.png)

To the API reference docs (http://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/APIReference/API_CreateTable.html):

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/368057/10589915/d6d3a216-7662-11e5-9274-f30d53e372b2.png)

The "Projection" list elements of KEYS_ONLY, INCLUDE, and ALL are not being rendered properly.    Note how KEYS_ONLY is in the description but not rendered as a sublist, and INCLUDE and ALL look like they're part of the same list as "Projection" but they're actually sub elements.

issue_title: Unable to use Cython-using packages that depend on other Cython-using packages
issue_body:
Hello! I'm trying to have `shiv` create a file that has the `asynq` package. `asynq` uses Cython, and depends on `qcore` (which also uses Cython).

When I try a naive `shiv asynq -o foo.shiv`, it fails to install because compiling `asynq` requires  `qcore`'s Cython-specific files. It seems that when `shiv` runs `pip install --target blah`, the information about the target is not passed down to Cython, so Cython is trying to look in the normal Python path for the `qcore` files instead of the temporary site-packages directory that we are actually installing things into.

Is there a supported solution for how to install packages that depend on other Cython packages like this?

If not, I made a janky proof of concept (https://github.com/linkedin/shiv/compare/master...kennydo:kedo-set-pythonpath) for getting this to work.
This branch:
1) sets `PYTHONPATH` when calling `pip` to the target `site-packages` directory (so that Cython is able to look in the right place for the `qcore` files) and
2) makes individual `pip install` calls (so that when we know that `qcore` has been installed by the time we try to compile `asynq`).
I was able to create a shiv successfully by running: `shiv qcore asynq -o foo.shiv`.

By default i18ndude extracts its messages through ``zope.tal``.

(Un)fortunately Plone 5 is moving to ``Chameleon``, so what will work perfectly on production and tests can possibly not work on i18ndude.

It just happened to me: I made an internal release of ``Chameleon`` with the ``i18n:ignore`` added on it and tests and production work perfectly but as I did not make a ``zope.tal`` internal release as well it still complains about them.

Unfortunately this ``five.pt``, ``z3c.pt`` and ``Chameleon`` provide a drop-in replacement for ``zope.tal`` but it's not exactly a 1:1 translation of code, so I'm not sure how easy it would be to make them exchangeable (if possible at all).

Het there 👋 

Spent some time fighting with an issue which was a result of my misunderstanding of the documentation. Probably, it makes sense to clarify it a bit. 

As it turned out, none of the examples of the function work as is, while copied and pasted to the console. For example, this case would quietly drop spans.

```
parent = tracer.trace("parent")     # has no parent span
child  = tracer.trace("child")      # is a child of a parent
child.finish()
parent.finish()

parent2 = tracer.trace("parent2")   # has no parent span
parent2.finish()
```

The reason can be found in logs, but logs themeselves are not easily accessible if you run dd-agent from Docker.

```
 dropping trace reason: invalid span ... span.normalize: empty Service (debug for more info)
```

My suggestion is to make it clear from the documentation that the span will be quietly dropped if the service is not set, and then modify the example to make it explicit the fact that service is needed at least for the top frame. To something like

```
parent = tracer.trace("parent", service="frontend")     # has no parent span
child  = tracer.trace("child")      # is a child of a parent
child.finish()
parent.finish()

parent2 = tracer.trace("parent2", service="frontend")   # has no parent span
parent2.finish()
```

In addition, maybe it could make sense to issue a warning on `finish()` for traces without services being set.

issue_title: No way to refund billing agreement transactions
issue_body:
As far as I can tell there's no way to refund billing agreement transactions using the PayPal REST API.  You can refund Sale objects of course, but I can't find any way to go from a billing agreement transaction ID to a Sale ID.  For reference, the transaction IDs I'm referring to come from code like this:

    billing_agreement = BillingAgreement.find(agreement_id, api=self.api)
    transactions =  billing_agreement.search_transactions(...)
    payments = transactions.agreement_transaction_list

Then each item in <payments> has a "transaction_id".  I'd like to be able to refund a particular transaction here via the REST API.

It is possible to reverse these using the classic API, which is what I'm doing now as a workaround:

    classic_interface.refund_transaction(transactionid=trans_id)

Now that it's possible to get payer info from an executed billing agreement this is the last thing my code does that still needs the classic API.  It would be so nice to go 100% REST and would save my clients a lot of trouble getting classic API keys.  

Is there a way to do this in REST, or if not could you add one?  

Thank you!

issue_title: How do I make commits with a specified date?
issue_body:
I can't seem to find out how to make commit with specifying `author_date` and `commit_date`, but it did not really work:

```
import git
...
repo = git.Repo.init(repo_path)
repo.index.add(['some_file'])
repo.index.commit(message, authored_date=commit_date, committed_date=commit_date)
```

throws:

    TypeError: commit() got an unexpected keyword argument 'authored_date'

Then I looked at the [source](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/master/git/objects/commit.py#L269) and found out it does not really have parameters for commit datetime. (or am I missing something? Would you be interested in a PR which adds these parameters? I am interested to learn and make contribution :smile: )

So, I either I have to create a commit then change dates (using [`Commit`](http://gitpython.readthedocs.org/en/stable/tutorial.html#the-commit-object) object) or use [git directly](http://gitpython.readthedocs.org/en/stable/tutorial.html#using-git-directly).

issue_title: stats value for evictions is boolean when it should be numeric?
issue_body:
per https://github.com/memcached/memcached/blob/b1debc4c96134e9014a929c32d6990cb5d66a22c/doc/protocol.txt#L562-L563 the value for `evictions` from a `stats` command is numeric.

but pymemcache is treating it as on/off: https://github.com/pinterest/pymemcache/blob/aac032419e94b5f71e763c130d54d4b6f36caa17/pymemcache/client/base.py#L56

this seems odd, because if memcached returns `evictions` as `55`, pymemcache is returning this as `False`.

i'd send a pull request, but it seems like this might have done for a reason that's escaping me at the moment ;) let me know.

issue_title: Is it a bug that consumer_timeout_ms doesn't work for BalancedConsumer?
issue_body:
I have set `consumer_timeout_ms` when I am getting a `BalancedConsumer` object.
```python
consumer = topic.get_balanced_consumer(
        'testgroup',
        consumer_timeout_ms=1000)
message = consumer.consume(block=True) # it blocks forever if there is no message
```

After some debugs, I found that `consumer_timeout_ms` works in the internal consumer. But 
it cann't exit the loop in the function `consume` of `BalancedConsumer`.
```python
def consume(self, block=True):
    """Get one message from the consumer

    :param block: Whether to block while waiting for a message
    :type block: bool
    """
    message = None
    while message is None:
        try:
            message = self._consumer.consume(block=block) 
        except ConsumerStoppedException:
            continue
        if not block:
            return message
    return message
```
After timeout, the internal consumer returns None, and the loop continues.

issue_body: The 'all' and 'any' modes are maybe not needed - one can just use 'exact' and check the result compared to 0 and 1. In fact, this is hard to implement internally because for 'all', the exact mode can sometimes give results are are extremely close but not exactly 1 - so then we have to introduce a tolerance, which gets messy. I'd actually vote to remove these modes and just keep 'center', 'subpixels', and 'exact'. What do others think?

In this issue I'd like to discuss if the current PRF extraction and application method makes sense or whether it can be replaced by an oversampled PSF estimation.

cc @astrofrog @bsipocz @larrybradley @adonath @eteq 

### Current method

@adonath implemented as part of his GSoC the [create_prf](http://photutils.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/photutils.create_prf.html) function, which creates a [DiscretePRF](http://photutils.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/photutils.DiscretePRF.html) object which is then used via [psf_photometry](http://photutils.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/photutils.psf_photometry.html) for PSF fitting photometry to estimate source fluxes (and optionally positions). A full example for Spitzer data analysis using this is [here](http://nbviewer.ipython.org/github/astropy/photutils-datasets/blob/master/notebooks/PSFPhotometrySpitzer.ipynb).

If I understand correctly the `DiscretePRF` class represents the PSF pre-sampled for a 2d grid of sub-pixel positions (i.e. 100 PSF images for an oversampling factor of 10), and the way PRF estimation in `create_prf` works is that only stars where the position falls into a given sub-pixel are used in `psf_photometry` when fitting data with that PRF.

### The problem

The problem with the current approach is that if the number of stars is limited, then there will be subpixels with few or no star to estimate the PSF for that subpixel, resulting in failing or poor `psf_photometry` results.

### A proposal

How about instead of a function `create_prf` that estimates a `DiscretePRF` model, we write a `create_psf` function that estimates a `DiscretePSF` model?
But instead of being a 4-dimensional array like `DiscretePRF` of shape `(nx, ny, n_subpix, n_subpix)` it's a 2-dimensional array of shape `(nx * n_subpix, ny * n_subpix)`, i.e. simply a high-resolution image of the PSF ... if I'm not mistaken that's also the format [psfex](http://www.astromatic.net/software/psfex) uses.
`create_psf` would re-sample the stars onto this high-resolution image and then `psf_photometry` would re-sample the high-resolution PSF image onto the actual data grid or pre-compute a PRF object if that is actually needed for speed.

The big difference would be that the PSF used in `psf_photometry` for every star would contain the info from every star available for PSF estimation, thus largely getting rid of the problem described above.

Am I missing something and there's a good use case for the current method that would not be covered by this alternative proposal? If so, can you please point me to a webpage or paper describing why it's better?

In the Python implementation of Spatial Pooler the method to find the global winners does so by [sorting the entire list of activations](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/src/nupic/research/spatial_pooler.py#L1427-L1429), and then taking the top k values. However, this is an inefficient approach. With n as the number of columns, sorting in general is O( n log n ), however it is possible to find the top k winners in only O( n ) time complexity. Using numpy, this can be accomplished using the [argpartition](http://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy-1.10.1/reference/generated/numpy.argpartition.html) function. Note that the [nupic.core implementation](https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/master/src/nupic/algorithms/SpatialPooler.cpp#L1163-L1167) uses a O( n ) approach that avoids sorting all the activations.

What follows is mostly mental notes re: specific implementation details of network api.

Contrary to `nupic.engine.Network`, which is a subclass and wrapper for the swig-generated `nupic.bindings.engine_internal.Network`, `nupic.engine.Region` is a standalone class with its own properties and behaviors independent of `nupiclbindings.engine_internal.Region`.  Consider the following example:

```python
region = network.addRegion("region1", "TestNode", "")
region1 = network.getRegions().getByName("region1")
```

You might infer from the signature of `Network.addRegion()` that `region` and `region1` would be the very same instance.  However, `region` is an instance of `nupic.engine.Region` whereas `region1` is an instance of `nupic.bindings.engine_internal.Region`.  The reason for this is `Network.addRegion()` as it is implemented at https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/bfa052f3d5740b3934c2dcefc566ab0906f9af2e/src/nupic/engine/__init__.py#L641-L646 calls `addRegion()` on the internal implementation, but then separately returns the result of `self._getRegions()[name]`.  `Network._getRegions()` wraps the result of `engine_internal.Network.getRegions()` such that the return value is another wrapper that implements a dict-like interface.

It seems like some of the complexity can be mitigated by implementing `Network.addRegion()` as something like the following:

```python
def addRegion(self, name, nodeType, nodeParams):
    """
    @doc:place_holder(Network.addRegion)
    """
    engine_internal.Network.addRegion(self, name, nodeType, nodeParams)
    return self.getRegions().getByName(name)
```

Or:

```python
def addRegion(self, name, nodeType, nodeParams):
    """
    @doc:place_holder(Network.addRegion)
    """
    return engine_internal.Network.addRegion(self, name, nodeType, nodeParams)
```

eea.jquery = 9.3

Before yesterday I was testing 10.0.dev0 from the git repo, but even after deleting it from my src folder, the actions are a no-show. 
I wonder if there is a setup import step (or several) that didn't get run? I don't see the actions in the zmi's portal_actions neither.

Would love to have it working! Any suggestion?

Thanks,

Ginet

Hi, thanks for writing chalice, it's sweet.

I would like to request making chalicelib optional in favor of explicit include/exclude paths to be deployed. This could certainly default to using chalicelib for deployment, but I think some people would prefer a more configurable/flexible solution.

With chalicelib/app.py as the only way to deploy your own code, you either have to add chalicelib to sys.path (so you can get relative imports, e.g. https://github.com/aws/chalice/issues/299), or litter your imports with "import chalicelib.yourlibrary", etc. Also, if you have optional files which should remain just in the dev deployment with .gitignore, there's no way to keep those in the chalicelib tree without deploying them. Sometimes keeping them out of chalicelib can be messy too.

I'm familiar with the discussion in https://github.com/aws/chalice/pull/146 and would like to vote for the explicit include/exclude option.
If space format is set, make it possible to use field aliases in the driver.

Now that the c++ side checks for c++ expressions I think it's possible to remove most instances of `ensure_freshness` in the cython code because python will catch the c++ exception.

That would probably make expression creation faster in python.

I noticed in the DyNet paper (https://arxiv.org/pdf/1701.03980v1.pdf) on page 19 that just-in-time parameter updates are mentioned, and Pegasos is cited. Two notes:

1 - My understanding is that AdaGrad does actually require an update to weights with zero gradient. However, they can also be implemented just-in-time as described in [http://aclweb.org/anthology/D/D15/D15-1032.pdf](http://aclweb.org/anthology/D/D15/D15-1032.pdf).
2 - I haven't seen it anywhere, but by approximating epsilon as 0 in Adam, I think a closed form update for weights with gradient zero can be formed.

Is there any interest in adding this to DyNet? I'm guessing it would be of the "we'd accept a pull request" variety :) but whether it is worth implementing depends on the anticipated impact on speed.

issue_title: Unable to create a new scan

issue_body:
Py-ART contains a number of kludges that are requires to support Python 2.6. Examples include [a lack of dictionary completions](https://github.com/ARM-DOE/pyart/blob/9088d22249f1952dd3665c703bb2e2710bfcc7f2/pyart/util/xsect.py#L108) and [no total_seconds datetime attribute](https://github.com/ARM-DOE/pyart/blob/9088d22249f1952dd3665c703bb2e2710bfcc7f2/pyart/io/mdv_common.py#L1066).

Dropping support for 2.6 would allow for simplier code and less work for future development.  Does any of out users have a continues need for Python 2.6 support?  I know @kirknorth used have a need for Python 2.6 support but I am not sure of his current requirements.  Please speak up now or format support will be dropped.

Looks like a space character made its way into the readthedocs link in the GitHub repository description:

![screenshot 2016-12-05 12 54 08](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3085290/20896030/f0dfb290-bae9-11e6-9619-fd8d051cfef3.png)

Link points to http://testtools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/%20 ; looks like it should be https://testtools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ .

(May as well make it https too 🙂)

issue_title: [Discussion] Is the distributed version control approach through memote relevant?
issue_body:
#### Problem description

> I don't believe this framework is general enough to be appropriate for the wide range of modeling tools that the community will develop. For example, Pathway Tools uses a database to store its models during development, not a text file, so a version control system is of no use to us. It is important to de-couple model distribution from model development. One might use SBML for model distribution but not use it for model development. In fact, does anyone really use SBML for model development? Do people hand edit XML files when developing models? This seems unlikely to me; people should be using higher level tools to develop their models that emit SBML when the model is ready to be published or distributed. So again I really don't see the distributed version control approach as relevant.
- Peter Karp, comment on the manuscript

> I can only speak from my personal experience, which, granted, might be quite limited in comparison to that of other co-authors. 
However, for my thesis' main project I started from an automated draft. Before I  was able to begin with the curation, I had to make several global changes to the .xml file, which I did by editing it directly. Then I curated the model in sets of cohesive steps with cobrapy, at the end of each set I would export the model to SMBL.
I agree that version control of a database is no simple task. As far as I'm aware Pathway Tools supports an SBML export. So in theory one could apply the same workflow of emitting intermittent versions of the database as SBML, which would make version control feasible. 
We had hoped that by choosing SBML as the primary format we would stay agnostic to the differences between each tool. Since memote doesn't introduce any changes to a model, a re-import is unnecessary. 
Could you explain why you think it may not be decoupled?

> [...] I would like to move this discussion to GitHub so that we can discuss this with the whole community. I think it would be quite valuable to learn exactly what the users habits are, and then take it from there.

- My initial responses on the manuscript

issue_title: Missing Six runtime dependency after pip install.
issue_body:
Performing `pip install healthcheck` or  `pip install -r /path/to/requirements.txt` with a requirements file containing `healthcheck==1.3.2` does not result in the install of six 1.10.0.

Running an application using healthcheck after such an install results in an invalid import

```
    from healthcheck import HealthCheck
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/healthcheck/__init__.py", line 4, in <module>
    import six
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'six'
```

Ideally the runtime dependency of `six` should be installed when installing healthcheck.

The body of the description is also inaccurate as it always does a PATCH request.
See: https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/latest/bigquery/reference.html#google.cloud.bigquery.client.Client.update_table

Today, our daily `gcloud ml-engine job` has been broken. The problem may come from the changes in the module _grpc-google-iam-v1_. It seems that you did an update for the module _grpc-google-iam-v1_ from **0.11.1** (?) to **0.11.3**  on the server-side.

issue_title: Consider a heartbeat/monitor helper for subscriber
issue_labels: [{'id': 139685153, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/api:%20pubsub', 'name': 'api: pubsub', 'color': 'f8f8f8', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604243, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/priority:%20p2', 'name': 'priority: p2', 'color': 'fef2c0', 'default': False}, {'id': 810248285, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/triaged%20for%20GA', 'name': 'triaged for GA', 'color': 'b5a1ea', 'default': False}, {'id': 77438582, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/type:%20feature%20request', 'name': 'type: feature request', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
It would extremely useful for users and for maintainers if pubsub had a sort of "heartbeat"/"monitor" helper. For example:

```python
subscription = subscriber.subscribe(...)
monitor = pubsub_v1.subscriber.Monitor(
    subscription,
    period=5 * 60  # Output once every five minutes.
)
monitor.start()

future = subscription.open()
future.result()
```

Every 5 minutes, the monitor would wake up and output the following to stderr:

```
# Pub/Sub Monitor for subscriber 0xDEADBEEF
subscription_name: /project/my-project/subscriptions/my-subscription
total_messages: 5000
ack_p99: 10s
outstanding_requests_p99: 10
...
```

`google.cloud.spanner.databse._BrokenOperationFuture` is a subclass of `google.gax._OperationFuture` which attempts to work around the fact that the spanner back-end sometimes returns metadata instead of the expected empty response, which causes the normal future to blow up.

Once the back-end is updated to fix this, remove `_BrokenOperationFuture`.

H/T to http://inre.dundeemt.com/2014-05-17/pypi-setup-py-keeping-a-dry-long_description/ (I was Googling around for DRY in `setup.py`)

```
python setup.py check --restructuredtext --strict
```

---

For example, running it on the post #2442 version gives:

```
$ python setup.py check --restructuredtext --strict
running check
warning: check: Duplicate implicit target name: "contributing".

warning: check: Duplicate implicit target name: "license".

error: Please correct your package.
```

because we have sections named license and contributing as well as links with those names. This could be fixed via

``` diff
diff --git a/README.rst b/README.rst
index 715368b..bbf0f72 100644
--- a/README.rst
+++ b/README.rst
@@ -96,16 +96,16 @@ Contributing

 Contributions to this library are always welcome and highly encouraged.

-See `CONTRIBUTING`_ for more information on how to get started.
+`See CONTRIBUTING`_ for more information on how to get started.

-.. _CONTRIBUTING: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.rst
+.. _See CONTRIBUTING: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.rst

 -------

-Apache 2.0 - See `LICENSE`_ for more information.
+Apache 2.0 - `See LICENSE`_ for more information.

-.. _LICENSE: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/LICENSE
+.. _See LICENSE: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/LICENSE
```

issue_title: allow stable_json_sha1() to handle dicts with non-string keys
issue_body:
See [this comment](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/6413#discussion_r213888866) on #6413. Essentially, the python docs [specifically state that json-ifying a dict will coerce the keys to strings](https://docs.python.org/2/library/json.html#json.dumps), but that doesn't end up happening for some reason (as in, it will always throw a `TypeError` -- this may be related to the fact that we use `sort_keys=True` in `stable_json_hash()`?).

We work around this in #6413 by calling `stable_json_sha1()` on all keys of any dict option we try to fingerprint before passing it to `stable_json_sha1()` (along with some other hacks, including wrapping every option we fingerprint in a list -- that one may not need fixing though). It would be wonderful if we could somehow do this in our json encoder class directly (in `options_fingerprinter.py`).

issue_title: introduce a way to turn a non-zero exit code for a process execution into a Throw
issue_body:
See @illicitonion's [comment on #5703](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/5703#discussion_r182137875):

> Maybe we should expose a error_on_nonzero_exit_code boolean on ExecuteProcessRequest (either in the Rust or in a Python wrapper) to do this automagically? It feels like 90% of ExecuteProcessRequests are probably going to want this behaviour, and it's going to be annoyingly much boilerplate to have to add it each time

This was in response to the below code:
```python
@rule(JavacVersionOutput, [Select(JavacVersionExecutionRequest)])
def get_javac_version_output(javac_version_command):
  javac_version_proc_req = yield Get(
    ExecuteProcessRequest, JavacVersionExecutionRequest, javac_version_command)
  javac_version_proc_result = yield Get(
    ExecuteProcessResult, ExecuteProcessRequest, javac_version_proc_req)

  # This part could be automated somehow.
  exit_code = javac_version_proc_result.exit_code
  if exit_code != 0:
    stdout = javac_version_proc_result.stdout
    stderr = javac_version_proc_result.stderr
    raise ProcessExecutionFailure(
      exit_code, stdout, stderr, 'obtaining javac version')

  yield JavacVersionOutput(
    version_output=javac_version_proc_result.stderr,
  )
```

@illicitonion suggested a modification to ExecuteProcessRequest which would perform this check (and raise e.g. ProcessExecutionFailure) if a flag was set. Personally, while we removed the Binary class in #5703, it seems to me like this kind of processing is where re-introducing the Binary base class could be useful, if the logic to generate arguments for process requests and to interpret an ExecuteProcessResult was co-located with the object that exposes a specific binary.

It's not clear what the right way to implement this should be, or what form it will take.

issue_title: Don't require "." prefix for dot commands
issue_body:
Additional discussion here: https://github.com/awslabs/aws-shell/issues/76

The idea is that similar to how the `%` isn't required in ipython (if automagic is enabled), we don't have to require the `.` prefix.  Additional notes:

* These commands would have the lowest precedence.  Conceptually we'd check to ensure there's no CLI command with the provided name, and then fall back to the dot command handler.
* If there's ever a CLI command with the same name as a dot command, you'd always have to specify the `.` prefix for the shadowed dot command.
* ipython has a config option to enable/disable this behavior.  Need to discuss if we want this configurable or just always enabled.

So my use case is mounting a remote folder, which contains several video files, with sshfs. Then making a connection to the video files with imageio.reader. When I close my program I want to close the video files and unmount the remote folder. However imageio.reader seems to be maintaining a connection to the file even after it's closed. Here is a minimal code example

```
import subprocess
import imageio
import os

mount_command = 'sshfs -o ro -o allow_other -o IdentityFile={} {}@{}:{} {}'.format(
		key_file, host_username, host_ip, host_data_directory, local_data_directory)

video = imageio.get_reader(video_file,  'ffmpeg')
video.close()
unmount_command = 'fusermount -u {}'.format(local_data_directory)
subprocess.call(unmount_command, shell=True)
```

Because `six==1.4.1` is embedded in the OSX system python, we get a conflict with our included version if someone does a `sudo pip install globus-sdk` on OSX. This is also described in #45
The result is that our six is installed behind the system version, and isn't the one loaded at import time.

Solutions that have been batted around include just doing doc about virtualenv ( #45 ), trying to modify `sys.path`, and moving off of `six` altogether.
Although the virtualenv is a best practice, and ultimately the safest solution, it's desirable to let people do the global pip install if we can support it without too much effort.
The push to do that becomes somewhat stronger when you consider that these dependencies are inherited by the CLI.

The current usage sites for `six` are as follows:

- `six.add_metaclass` on base Authorizer
- `six.moves.configparser` in config loading
- `six.moves.urllib.parse` in a couple of locations
- `six.Iterator` as a parent of `PaginatedResource`

The use of `six.moves` is pretty easy to work around, since the things that are renamed generally haven't changed their signatures. Even if they have, we can wrap methods with our own helpers to handle this.
[six.Iterator](https://pythonhosted.org/six/#six.Iterator) is a convenience like the `six.moves` module, so that's also not a big deal.

If we had meaningful things in `six.add_metaclass`, this could get really painful trying to define 2-to-3 compatible metaclasses, but we're just using it to make an abstract base class non-instantiable.
That's not really important -- having all of its methods raise `NotImplementedError` communicates the same thing.

Unless there's a gotcha somewhere in this, we should just go ahead and do it.
This does *not* obsolete #45 -- that's still a best practice and we should encourage it.

issue_title: Latin Extended-A characters being converted to ASCII on INSERT
issue_body:
If I insert a Latin Extended-A character into my MS SQL Server database, it appears to be getting converted to its ASCII equivalent character.

Here's my setup:
Python 3.4.4, running on CentOS 6.6
MS SQL Server 2008 R2
Using the Microsoft ODBC Driver 11 for SQL Server on Linux with unixODBC 2.3.2

```sql
create table dbo.turbodbc (
  col_int int null,
  col_str_a varchar(100) null,  -- ascii (single-byte per character) column
  col_str_u nvarchar(100) null  -- unicode (actually UCS2 LE, two bytes per character) column
)
```
```python
# the following string has three test characters separated by spaces:
#   a normal "ascii" character c, hex value \x63
#   c-cedilla, from "Latin-1 Supplement" but still a one byte value \xE7
#   c-with-an-acute-accent, from "Latin Extended-A", i.e. needs two bytes, value \x0107
# see http://inamidst.com/stuff/unidata/ for reference
string = 'c \xe7 \u0107'
row = [1, string, string]
sql = "insert into dbo.turbodbc (col_int, col_str_a, col_str_u) values (?, ?, ?)"
crsr = conn.cursor()
crsr.execute(sql, row)
conn.commit()
```
If I query for the hex equivalent of the two columns in the table, I get: `0x6320E72063` and `0x63002000E70020006300`.  As you can see, the third test character has been converted from c-with-an-acute-accent to plain old c, in both columns. I can kind-of understand this would happen in the first (ascii) column because the column can contain only single-byte characters, but the second "unicode" column should be able to handle this and hence contain `0x63002000E70020000701`, i.e. end with `0701` not `6300` (note the little-endianness).

Somewhere along the line, the "Latin Extended-A" character \u0107 is being translated to \u0063, even if the target column is a unicode column.  Would this be happening within turbodbc?  Or somewhere else?  Is turbodbc designed to work only with single-byte characters?

issue_title: trinity attach command should check for existing IPC handles and exit cleanly if it can't find them
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

This is a follow-up to the discussion in https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/issues/950.
The `trinity attach` command currently doesn't recognize when it fails to attach to a running `trinity` instance leaving users with unhandled exceptions upon `w3` usage.

### How can it be fixed

Check if we find the IPC handles that `trinity attach` assumes to exist. If we don't find them, print a user friendly message and exit with error code.

When using the `spawn` method (our default) for the multiprocessing module, I often get a bunch of unhandled KeyboardInterrupt exceptions when I Ctrl+C trinity. If using `TRINITY_MP_CONTEXT=fork` I never seem to get those

```
    INFO  06-04 15:06:13       chain  Imported 192 headers in 2.25 seconds, new head: #1346212 (1cd32f)                                                                         
^C    INFO  06-04 15:06:14        main  Keyboard Interrupt: Stopping                                                                                                            
Process Process-2:3:                                                                                                                                                            
Process Process-2:1:                                                                                                                                                            
    INFO  06-04 15:06:14         ipc  Sent SIGINT to process 27860, waiting 5 seconds for it to terminate                                                                       
Process Process-2:2:                                                                                                                                                            
Traceback (most recent call last):                                                                                                                                              
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/process.py", line 258, in _bootstrap                                                                                                 
    self.run()                                                                                                                                                                  
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/process.py", line 93, in run                                                                                                         
    self._target(*self._args, **self._kwargs)                                                                                                                                   
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/concurrent/futures/process.py", line 169, in _process_worker                                                                                         
    call_item = call_queue.get(block=True)                                                                                                                                      
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/queues.py", line 93, in get                                                                                                          
    with self._rlock:                                                                                                                                                           
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/synchronize.py", line 96, in __enter__                                                                                               
    return self._semlock.__enter__()                                                                                                                                            
KeyboardInterrupt                                                                                                                                                               
Traceback (most recent call last):                                                                                                                                              
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/process.py", line 258, in _bootstrap                                                                                                 
    self.run()                                                                                                                                                                  
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/process.py", line 93, in run                                                                                                         
    self._target(*self._args, **self._kwargs)                                                                                                                                   
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/concurrent/futures/process.py", line 169, in _process_worker                                                                                         
    call_item = call_queue.get(block=True)                                                                                                                                      
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/queues.py", line 94, in get                                                                                                          
    res = self._recv_bytes()                                                                                                                                                    
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/connection.py", line 216, in recv_bytes                                                                                              
    buf = self._recv_bytes(maxlength)                                                                                                                                           
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/connection.py", line 407, in _recv_bytes                                                                                             
    buf = self._recv(4)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/connection.py", line 379, in _recv
    chunk = read(handle, remaining)
KeyboardInterrupt
```

it looks like the kind of thing that would be caused by the [multiprocessing module](https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/multiprocessing.html#multiprocessing.Semaphore):

> If the SIGINT signal generated by Ctrl-C arrives while the main thread is blocked by a call to BoundedSemaphore.acquire(),
Lock.acquire(), RLock.acquire(), Semaphore.acquire(), Condition.acquire() or Condition.wait() then the call will be immediately
interrupted and KeyboardInterrupt will be raised.

issue_title: apply_key_map behaviour is ambiguous if remapping would overwrite other field.
issue_body:
* Version: 1.0


### What was wrong?

The `apply_key_map` function can be both non-deterministic as well as ambiguous to what the proper behavior is when a remapped key collides with a non-remapped key.

```python
>>> apply_key_map({'a': 'b'}, {'b': 3})
{'b': 3}

>>> apply_key_map({'a': 'b'}, {'a': 2, 'b': 3})
{'b': 3}

>>> apply_key_map({'a': 'b'}, collections.OrderedDict((('a', 2), ('b', 3))))
{'b': 3}

>>> apply_key_map({'a': 'b'}, collections.OrderedDict((('b', 3), ('a', 2))))
{'b': 2}
```

### How can it be fixed?

I think the proper behavior to fix both of these issue is to do the following check

```python
key_conflicts = set(  # get the keys from the starting value
    original_value.keys()
).difference(  # remove any keys that are going to be remapped
    key_map.keys()
).intersection(  # find the intersection with the result of the remap.
    v 
    for k, v 
    in key_map.items() 
    if k in original_value
)
```

If there are any key conflicts then refuse to apply the remapping, raising a `KeyError` indicating what keys were in conflict.
