issue_title: conda develop error using Python from another channel (conda-forge)
issue_body:
I don't know if this is related to conda or conda-forge. I want to use conda to setup an environment so that I can work on the development of a Python package which is available on the conda-forge channel, e.g.,

```
$ conda create -n xarray_dev xarray -c conda-forge
$ source activate xarray_dev
(xarray_dev)$ conda remove xarray
```

```
(xarray_dev)$ git clone https://github.com/benbovy/xarray my_xarray_fork
```

So far so good. However, I get the following error when I run the conda develop command:

```
(xarray_dev)$ conda develop ./my_xarray_fork
Error: python is not installed in /Users/benbovy/miniconda3/envs/xarray_dev
```

It's weird because I'm sure that Python is installed in that environment. A current workaround is to re-install the same version of Python available from the default channel:

```
(xarray_dev)$ conda install python=3.5
(xarray_dev)$ conda develop ./my_xarray_fork     # it works
```

issue_title: [Bug] conda removes folders from working directory when they share name with environment
issue_body:
conda removes folders from working directory when they share their name with the environment that you want to delete. You can find a minimal reproduction at the tagged commit linked here:

https://github.com/Korijn/condabug/tree/conda-env-remove
# Steps to reproduce

Run the following command from the root of the repository:

``` batch
> conda env create
> conda remove -n hw --all -y
```

_Conda has now deleted not only the `hw` environment but also the `hw` source folder!_

Since `conda env remove`'s behavior was redirect to the same code in a fairly recent release, you can also reproduce the bug like this:

``` batch
> conda env create
> conda env remove -n hw -y
```

You can even cause the behavior after the environment has already been deleted.

blacklist:

issue_title: NoneType error: `az storage entity query --table-name mytable`
issue_body:
# Describe the bug
Command `az storage entity query --table-name "mytable"` returns:
```
az storage entity query: error: 'NoneType' object is not iterable
```

This appears to be affecting versions:
- `azure-cli=2.0.32-1~wheezy`
- `azure-cli=2.0.31-1~wheezy`

32-1 appears to have been removed, but 31-1 is still serving from repo. I was not able to successfully get the command run until downgraded to 30-1.

## Workaround
We install Azure CLI to VSTS Hosted Linux Agent(s) - so "clean" environments. We have to regress two versions:

```
apt-get update -y
apt-get install -y python libssl-dev libffi-dev python-dev build-essential jq tree
curl -L https://azurecliprod.blob.core.windows.net/install.py -o install.py
printf "/usr/azure-cli\n/usr/bin" | python install.py

apt-get update -y
apt-get install -y azure-cli=2.0.30-1~wheezy --allow-downgrades
```

# To Reproduce
1. Install latest azure-cli:
```
apt-get update -y
apt-get install -y python libssl-dev libffi-dev python-dev build-essential jq tree
curl -L https://azurecliprod.blob.core.windows.net/install.py -o install.py
printf "/usr/azure-cli\n/usr/bin" | python install.py
```
2. Export AZURE_STORAGE_CONNECTION_STRING for valid table storage account/table.
2. Run command: `az storage entity query --table-name "mytable"`
2. Observe error.

## Expected behavior
Return the contents of the table

# Environment summary
Env: Ubuntu 16.04.4 LTS
Env2: VSTS hosted Linux Agent

Install Method:
```
apt-get update -y
apt-get install -y python libssl-dev libffi-dev python-dev build-essential jq tree
curl -L https://azurecliprod.blob.core.windows.net/install.py -o install.py
printf "/usr/azure-cli\n/usr/bin" | python install.py
```

We should support the ability to list files in a path (without navigating the directory object structure) like the `azure storage file list` command:

```
$ azure storage file list --help
help:    List storage files in the specified share under specific path
help:    
help:    Usage: storage file list [options] [share] [path]
help:    
help:    Options:
help:      -h, --help                                  output usage information
help:      -v, --verbose                               use verbose output
help:      --json                                      use json output
help:      --share <share>                             the storage share name
help:      -p, --path <path>                           the path to be listed
help:      --sas <sas>                                 the shared access signature of the storage file share
help:      -a, --account-name <accountName>            the storage account name
help:      -k, --account-key <accountKey>              the storage account key
help:      -c, --connection-string <connectionString>  the storage connection string
help:      -vv                                         run storage command in debug mode
help:    
help:    Current Mode: arm (Azure Resource Management)

```

According to https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/azure/hh264517.aspx
REST API returns a Response Header with the request-id.
Then it is possible to track the operation status like any other async op (as mentioned in the same doc).

In delete_storage_account() patching the request from:
```
return self._perform_delete(
            self._get_storage_service_path(service_name))
```
to
```
return self._perform_delete(
            self._get_storage_service_path(service_name), async=True)
```
should to the trick.

PR on the way.

issue_title: `ClientSession.close` deprecated when called directly via run_until_complete(2.2.3)
issue_body:
## Long story short

When you close session directly via `loop.run_until_complete(session.close())`, it is deprecated.
The reason is that `_CoroGuard` inherits from `_BaseCoroMixin` that inherits from `collections.abc.Coroutine`.
 https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/v2.2.3/aiohttp/helpers.py#L108
And when calling `loop.run_until_complete(session.close())` then `__await__` or `__iter__` methods are unreachable, `send` method is called instead. That's why, `self._awaited` attribute is not changed to `True`.

## Expected behaviour
Direct closing `loop.run_until_complete(session.close())` is not deprecated

## Actual behaviour

Direct closing `loop.run_until_complete(session.close())` is deprecated

## Steps to reproduce

```python
import asyncio  
from aiohttp import ClientSession

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()

session = ClientSession(loop=loop)
loop.run_until_complete(session.close())
```

## Your environment

```
OS X 10.12.3
python 3.6.0
aiohttp==2.2.3
```

issue_title: Revisit our choice to use Doct and document our reasoning
issue_body:
@tacaswell and I last revisited this, by phone, about six months ago. One can make a case for returning plain dicts, as RunEngine does, but ultimately we decided it wasn't a strong enough case. The original motivations for creating doct were immutability (of which it does a less-than-perfect job) and adding getattr-based access as a synonym for getitem-based access, which is convenient but has downsides.

If we decide to stick with doct (which I suspect we will) then we should document our reasoning somewhere.

<VirtualHost *:443>
	ServerName example.com
	ServerAlias www.example.com
<If "%{HTTP_HOST} != 'www.example.com'">
	Redirect "/" "https://www.example.com/"
</If>
	DocumentRoot /home/example/www/example.com
	Include /etc/letsencrypt/options-ssl-apache.conf
	SSLCertificateFile /etc/letsencrypt/live/example.com/cert.pem
	SSLCertificateKeyFile /etc/letsencrypt/live/example.com/privkey.pem
	SSLCertificateChainFile /etc/letsencrypt/live/example.com/chain.pem
</VirtualHost>
````
which is a much better solution.

issue_title: Wrong dst() value for time in Portugal tz
issue_body:
Pick a time in the Summer of 1996:
```
>>> t = datetime(1996, 6, 1, tzinfo=tz.gettz('Portugal'))
```
the time zone abbreviation is computed correctly:
```
>>> t.tzname()
'WEST'
```
([WEST](http://www.timeanddate.com/time/zones/west) = Western European Summer Time).

However, 

```
>>> t.dst()
datetime.timedelta(0)
```

and as a consequence:

```
>>> t.timetuple().tm_isdst
0
>>> from time import strftime
>>> strftime('%Z', t.timetuple())
'WET'
```

The problem is that `t.dst()` is computed as a difference between `t.utcoffset()` and the offset of the last standard time before `t`, but Portugal switched to `WEST` in the Spring of 1996 from `CET`, not `WET` and therefore UTC offset did not change, while isdst did.

I came across the [RFC 7529](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7529#section-4) while trying to investigate a case where a monthly rrule would skip over February with a dtstart on the 30 / 31 of a previous month.

I understand the expected behavior of rrule under the current implementation is to ignore values which are invalid dates, but I was wondering if support for RFC 7529 was planned or would be accepted if properly implemented in a PR.

Thanks for the work on dateutil anyway, this module is a lifesaver !

The [`REQUESTS_MAX_RETRIES`](https://github.com/django-salesforce/django-salesforce/blob/3597d1dcb0f3fea377a1d32f9fc0e554a9dd5759/salesforce/backend/__init__.py#L31) setting is used to retry failed requests.

I saw an issue when creating new SObjects where a `ReadTimeoutError` occurred while connecting to the SalesForce API.

The SObject was created on SalesForce's side, but requests retried the request ([as expected](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/9e9d2c6521e37e1c4f35e04f95a910ad47e28958/requests/adapters.py#L88)), which resulted in a duplicate SObject being created in SalesForce.

I'd like to see `REQUESTS_MAX_RETRIES` documented, and perhaps differing behavior between retrying GET requests, but not retrying POST/PUT/DELETE requests automatically in the SalesForce database backend.

I've changed `REQUESTS_MAX_RETRIES` to `0` in all of my projects using `django-salesforce` (which is also the [default in requests](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/9e9d2c6521e37e1c4f35e04f95a910ad47e28958/requests/adapters.py#L47))

To simulate/mock a real device, it would be very useful to be able to define custom responses to transmitted messages, so to be able to (unit)test an application without having a real device present. A good way to do that would be via the `loop://`variant of `serialfor_url()`, with an additional kwarg `responses`.
If a regular dictionary is given, and a message is written to the device, if the message is a key of the dict, the respective value is placed in the read buffer (if that is the correct term) to be read back. If the message is not in the dict, it will just be played back as usual (that could be dependent on a `strict` flag or somesuch)
If a function/method is passed as `responses`, that function is passed the value that was written, and it returns some other value to be placed in the read buffer. This enables a more or less arbitrary amount of fidelity/sophistication in the behaviour of the simulated device.
I think this functionality would be quite useful, and `serialfor_url('loop:///')` would be a natural fit, but an additional variant would also be thinkable if you prefer that.

Simple doctests:
```
>>> import serial
>>> resp = {'V1 10': 'OK', 'IDN': 'my_id'}
>>> ser = serial.serial_for_url('loop://', responses=resp)
>>> ser.write('V1 10')
>>> ser.rear(10)
OK
>>> ser.write('bla')
>>> ser.read(10)
bla
>>> ser.close()
```
```
>>> import serial
>>> ser = serial.serial_for_url('loop://', responses=lambda x: 'Reply: ' + x)
>>> ser.write('V1 10')
>>> ser.read(20)
Reply: V1 10
>>> ser.write('bla')
>>> ser.read(10)
Reply: bla
>>> ser.close()
```

```
>>> import serial
>>> class DeviceMock:
...     def __init__():
...         <snip>
...     def reply(request):
...         <complex logic here>
...         return somevalue
>>> my_responses = DeviceMock()  
>>> ser = serial.serial_for_url('loop://', responses=my_responses.reply)
>>> ser.write('V1 10')
>>> ser.read(20)
Successfully set V1!
>>> ser.write('V1 10')
>>> ser.read(20)
V1 already at 10!
>>> ser.close()
```

Would you agree that this is a useful feature? Would you welcome a PR for this?

issue_title: windows or mac platform has  no lasagne v0.2, can you update the version to v0.2. Because I find so much library relys on v0.2, thank you very much.
issue_body:
Before submitting your issue, please check these hints!

- **If you have a usage question**, please please post on [our mailing list](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!forum/lasagne-users) instead of creating an issue.
  Make sure to check the [Lasagne documentation](http://lasagne.readthedocs.org/en/latest/) and the [Theano documentation](http://deeplearning.net/software/theano/) first!
  You can search the mailing list as well to see if your question has come up before.

- **If you suspect you have found a bug**, please first try [updating to the bleeding-edge versions of Theano and Lasagne](http://lasagne.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user/installation.html#bleeding-edge-version). It may have been fixed already.
  If you are not sure whether the problem lies within your code, Theano, or Lasagne, first post on [our mailing list](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!forum/lasagne-users).
  In any case, try to provide a minimal code example that reproduces the bug, this will greatly speed up the process of tracking down the problem.

- **If you have a feature request or idea**, please include a clear description of the use case(s) it would enable, referencing research papers if applicable, and indicate whether you would be willing to implement the feature yourself.
  We are happy to discuss your suggestion, help refining it, and decide upfront whether it would fit the main library or our [Lasagne/Recipes](https://github.com/Lasagne/Recipes).

issue_title: Table.append should not accept arbitrary iterables
issue_body:
Currently, Table.append takes in an iterable that should represent a row in the table, and it maps elements of the iterable onto columns in the table to create a new row. This works well for lists or tuples, but if a string is passed in, the method puts a character of the string in each column. This can be confusing for students who don't understand this behavior. 

It would be better to check that the argument is an iterable of a type that makes sense to use as a row. Below are screenshots from a Piazza post about this issue.

issue_title: Excessive Delay when polling vehicle.attitude, etc.
issue_body:
I am working on a multi-agent fixed wing UAV formation project. The agents share information over UDP broadcast via a raspberry pi v3B. In the main control thread, I poll various attributes (attitude, position, channels, etc) and am using roll angle as a test case for debugging.

I have found (by profiling) that  the program spends the overwhelming majority of time waiting (to have a reasonable sampling time). This is expected.  I have found that as the other threads consume resources (not many resources,  but some), the reads, to, say, vehicle.attitude.roll produce values that are delayed by up to about 1.5 seconds. The resulting values are generally unique, but lag behind real-time by  that amount.

I dug into the code, and it looks like a queue is being used in mavlink.py to get messages from pymavlink to dronekit. I suspect this queue is not being emptied fast enough. If this is, in fact, a likely cause, what is the cleanest way manually process all messages in this queue to get real-time data into the vehicle object?

issue_title: Avoid use of install script for /cvmfs and use pip instead
issue_body:
We should retire the old install_script that is used for installation of the ganga version on /cvmfs. This will ensure that we have the identical libraries used everywhere and also will break our last dependence on /afs.

The best will be to implement this will be to install ganga inside a virtualenv on /cvmfs. However, we should provide a wrapper that means that the use of the virtualenv is not visible. I think something as simple as

```
#!/bin/sh
. <installdir>/bin/activate
ganga
```

should work. Would be a good idea to implement before #1229 .

issue_title: schema.execute with return_promise=True should consistently return promise.
issue_body:
If I specify `return_promise=True` to schema.execute, I expect to be able to `await` the result:
```python
result = await schema.execute(
    query,
    executor=graphql_asyncio.AsyncioExecutor(loop=loop),
    return_promise=True,
)
```

Unfortunately when a field unexpected to the schema is passed in a query, graphql returns the result object directly, resulting in `TypeError: object ExecutionResult can't be used in 'await' expression`. This means instead I have to write something more like this:

```python
import inspect

result = schema.execute(
    query,
    executor=graphql_asyncio.AsyncioExecutor(loop=loop),
    return_promise=True,
)

if inspect.isawaitable(result):  # yuck!
    result = await result
```

It looks like the issue is in [`graphql.py`](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/29aba38cb8b895aafe22168cf74afb392df8ab01/graphql/graphql.py#L41), which will return validation results and any other unexpected exception directly as an `ExecutionResult`. Does it make sense for this code to check `return_promise` as well to conditionally return a promise, to ensure a more consistent return type?

https://github.com/graphql/graphql-js/commit/56f0b39911101400e462def6880f90d1117f242c

I'm working on this.

issue_title: Speed-up builds by not using AppVeyor matrix feature
issue_body:
The matrix feature creates separate jobs for each platform, which are then queued. Queuing time on AppVeyor can be significant for a single job and is multiplied with multiple non-parallel jobs. In order to reduce queuing time, and thus the time for AppVeyor to complete an overall build, the number of jobs has to be reduced. 

It is possible to manually matrix tasks, set environment variables appropriately, and thus to run all the tox tests and build tasks within a single job.

Downsides to this manual matrix, or "linear" approach, include less clean code and potential for error with manually duplicating code blocks. These can be reduced through tidy indentation and spacing of code. The main upside is performance. Tests have shown that the linear approach can perform the same build tasks in 41% of the time of the matrix approach.
It seems as if no coverage data is being reported to coveralls because Travis isn't configured to run coverage

We have enabled debug toolbar on our test environment of Django 1.10.17. After auditing the site we have found that template_origin variable is not correctly sanitized. I understand this is a Debug addon that probably isn't aimed for production environments, but I wanted only to report this just as an informative post. 
Maybe this is the intended behavior of this variable. If this is correct, sorry for this post!

Proof of concept:

http://FQDN/__debug__/template_source/?template=pages/home.html&template_origin=/etc/passwd

![screenshot from 2017-08-09 14-36-37](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7797734/29136999-50ddf02e-7d15-11e7-8f14-916671582187.png)


Affected file: 

panels/templates/views.py

Affected portion of code:

`def template_source(request):
    """
    Return the source of a template, syntax-highlighted by Pygments if
    it's available.
    """
    template_origin_name = request.GET.get('template_origin', None)
    if template_origin_name is None:
        return HttpResponseBadRequest('"template_origin" key is required')
    template_name = request.GET.get('template', template_origin_name)

    final_loaders = []
    loaders = Engine.get_default().template_loaders

    for loader in loaders:
        if loader is not None:
            # When the loader has loaders associated with it,
            # append those loaders to the list. This occurs with
            # django.template.loaders.cached.Loader
            if hasattr(loader, 'loaders'):
                final_loaders += loader.loaders
            else:
                final_loaders.append(loader)

    for loader in final_loaders:
        if Origin:  # django>=1.9
            origin = Origin(template_origin_name)
            try:
                source = loader.get_contents(origin)
                break
            except TemplateDoesNotExist:
                pass
        else:  # django<1.9
            try:
                source, _ = loader.load_template_source(template_name)
                break
            except TemplateDoesNotExist:
                pass
    else:
        source = "Template Does Not Exist: %s" % (template_origin_name,)

    try:
        from pygments import highlight
        from pygments.lexers import HtmlDjangoLexer
        from pygments.formatters import HtmlFormatter

        source = highlight(source, HtmlDjangoLexer(), HtmlFormatter())
        source = mark_safe(source)
        source.pygmentized = True
    except ImportError:
        pass
`
Regards,

issue_title: PReLU alphas should be channel not pixel wise
issue_body:
I think the current implementation of the PReLU layer initializes its alpha parameters for each pixel but as shown in the corresponding [paper](http://arxiv.org/pdf/1502.01852v1.pdf) it should be channel wise. Pixel wise initialization also restricts the input size during inference. 

If this is indeed an error I am happy to submit a pull request.

issue_title: LSTM with more than one layer fails with "Cannot feed value ..."
issue_body:
I'm having an issue where I'm able to train a single layer LSTM without problem but adding a second layer results in a ValueError:

Single layer example:
```
net = tflearn.input_data([None,1000,4])
net = tflearn.lstm(net,128,dynamic=True,return_seq=False)
net = tflearn.fully_connected(net,1,activation='linear')
adam_opt = tflearn.Adam(learning_rate=0.001,epsilon=0.0001)
net = tflearn.regression(net,optimizer=adam_opt,loss='mean_square')
model =tflearn.DNN(net,tensorboard_verbose=3)
model.fit(ss[:-int(N/10)],pirs[:,:-int(N/10)].T,validation_set=(ss[-int(N/10):],pirs[:,-int(N/10):].T),show_metric=True,batch_size=256)
```

If I try adding another LSTM layer like this:

```
net = tflearn.input_data([None,1000,4])
net = tflearn.lstm(net,128,return_seq=True,dynamic=True)
net = tflearn.lstm(net,128,dynamic=True,return_seq=False)
net = tflearn.fully_connected(net,1,activation='linear')
adam_opt = tflearn.Adam(learning_rate=0.001,epsilon=0.0001)
net = tflearn.regression(net,optimizer=adam_opt,loss='mean_square')
model =tflearn.DNN(net,tensorboard_verbose=3)
model.fit(ss[:-int(N/10)],pirs[:,:-int(N/10)].T,validation_set=(ss[-int(N/10):],pirs[:,-int(N/10):].T),show_metric=True,batch_size=256)
```

I get the following error when trying to train:
`ValueError: Cannot feed value of shape (256, 1) for Tensor u'TargetsData/Y:0', which has shape '(1000, 1)'`

I'm used to TFLearn getting the dimensions right automatically, so this is a strange error.

What am I doing wrong?

Hello,

I really like this project and have started to use it with multiple projects. However, this can quickly lead to a drop-downs with a considerable number of different environments from different projects. A colleague and myself have started a slightly hacky approach in implement subfolder support which works for viewing and loading files from different subfolders. Before we continue implementing support for saving into subfolders we thought we ask if something like this already exists or is under development?

Currently we created a second drop down that allows the selection of folders as well as linking that back to server.py to change the PATH in which the server is looking for JSON files.

For me this seems to be a critical feature when used with multiple people/projects so I would be curious to hear what solutions other people have found. I guess another one could be to start different visdom servers for each project with different ports?

Best wishes,
Florian

I looked into a few libraries for fastText, but an essential question come up. I don't know which version of original fastText they correspond to and whether they are compatible with the latest version of fastText. It helps users a lot to make regular releases (and best for other language binds such as Python and Java as well).

It would be helpful to have the option to add a prefix to the column names output by `csvjoin` for easy disambiguation between similarly named columns. For example, I might have two CSV files `a.csv` and `b.csv` which both have a column `foo`. It would be nice to be able to do:

```
csvjoin --columns bar,bar --prefix a,b a.csv b.csv > c.csv
```

And then get the header `a_foo,a_bar,b_foo,b_bar`. This is especially useful when you have to do further processing on the file. It's meach easier to prepend a string to the column name instead of keeping track of indices. If this is something you're open to, I could work on a PR as I don't imagine it will take a lot of work.

issue_title: distributed TF IDF with Multinomial Naive Bayes
issue_body:
would be state of the art and very useful if Dask could natively handle distributed TF IDF matrices as input to a multinomial naive bayes model. I know this is a difficult problem to solve because for most implementations of computing TF IDF you need the entire Term Document matrix in memory so I'm not sure know how to solve this problem tbh. 
Problem referenced here: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/25145552/tfidf-for-large-dataset

issue_title: Redefined test function names causes some tests to not run
issue_body:
The following files include test function names multiple times, i.e. the function is redefined. This causes pytest to only run one of the two test functions.

- test_completion.py

This isn't really a bug but it can happen and I think there should be a warning about it for windows users. One of the windows system headers, "minwindef.h", defines the macros min() and max() which then mess with code in cast.h which uses the std::numeric_limits min() and max() functions. It took me forever to figure out why I got a syntax error when trying to bind a function with integer arguments and couldn't find any help online. I think it would be great if you guys made a check to undefine those macro's if they're there or warn windows users to include the pybind11.h header first.

issue_title: Upgrading to 2 leads to: Unknown/invalid class_ template parameters provided

issue_body:
One of the major commands letsencrypt needs to support is "rekey", which causes the issuance of new certificates with new keys, and probably revocation of the old ones.

A lot of people will want a "reissue" verb but the problem with that is that it really isn't clear if there should be a new key or not.

issue_title: Test the manual plugin with the DNS challenge in integration tests
issue_body:
I couldn't make this work. Boulder has an endpoint you can POST during testing to create a TXT as seen [here](https://github.com/letsencrypt/boulder/blob/1c1449b2841d74199689a964befcfdac24597a0e/test/js/test.js#L342). Kuba got this working as a test for ACME [here](https://github.com/kuba/letsencrypt/blob/dns-01/acme/tests/dns_test_client.py). I tried to mimic this, but instead used a simple shell script for the DNS challenge. The script was:
```
#!/bin/sh
curl -X POST 'http://localhost:8055/set-txt' -d \
    "{\"host\": \"_acme_challenge.$CERTBOT_DOMAIN.\", \
     \"value\": \"$CERTBOT_VALIDATION\"}"
```

Got the following feedback from someone who had tried to use certbot, but couldn't due to complications:

there's nothing specifically wrong with let's encrypt, it's more of a function of our infrastructure. in order to validate our domain with let's encrypt, we need to periodically (every ~3 months) pass an acme challenge by hosting a file they give us. this is relatively harder when using kubernetes, or any other container orchestration framework, because containers are scheduled across a variety of nodes depending on their resource constraints. each container usually does only performs a single role (e.g. run the web service, run nginx, run a single background job, etc.), so it's not as easy as just adding a cron job like the documentation says

one option is to spin up another container whose job it is to update the cert periodically, serve the acme challenge on some local server, link it to the nginx container, and have nginx proxy for that local server

or share a filesystem mount between the two containers, but that could be problematic because containers are separated across hosts (though kubernetes specifically does have a good abstraction for this)

another option is to directly integrate with nginx or haproxy by using their scripting extensions. for instance, nginx has the lua open resty module that lets you dynamically request certs and cache them within nginx, which is super nice because it doesn't involve any additional coordination between containers or host mounts

but there's no standard open resty image that does that for you easily. I spent a good few days writing and configuring one for my personal websites, and got it working, but imo it's not worth the effort right now

and that's really the ultimate issue with all of this - it's definitely possible, but it takes a good amount of time, and the other option is clicking a few buttons and getting a cert for 1 year or longer
so to let us focus on development and not ssl configuration, just buying a cert at this stage for 1 or more years makes more sense imo

questions:
does the plugin automatically request and renew certs for virtual hosts? will they be providing a docker image that has that functionality built it? does nginx need to be compiled with certain extensions (e.g. lua) to work with that functionality? how does it persist the certs? will the certs get cycled every time containers are restarted or rescheduled?

issue_title: Write a test for the netting capabilities
issue_body:
One nice property of raiden is, that the channel between Alice and Bob forms a *netting* channel. That means that Alice can send payments to Bob exceeding her initial deposit:

`A.sent > A.deposit`

the condition for this to work is that Bob has also sent payments to Alice in the meantime, so that

`B.sent >= (A.sent - A.deposit)`.

I.e.:
```
A.deposit = 10
B.deposit = 10
A.send(10)  # at this point Alice's deposit is exhausted
A.send(5) --> fail
B.send(5)  # this replenishes the available tokens for Alice
A.send(5)  # is now possible
```

At settlement, Alice and Bob will receive their respective *netted balances*.

We should have a **simple** test case that exemplifies and ensure's this behavior (I couldn't find one on first glance through https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/tests).

issue_title: Add range filter in count/search/aggregate API
issue_body:
In gitlab by @sylvaingaudan on Jul 17, 2017, 17:03

Add `range` filter for count/search/aggregate operations.

A range is defined by:
- a field
- an inclusive or exclusive start
- an inclusive or exclusive end

If some ranges are defined on a same field, then an OR operator is defined between those ranges and only those ranges, AND otherwise.

issue_title: Use hiredis to decode redis outputs for python 3.
issue_body:
In Python3, using the standard Redis Client will encode all strings as a byte literal. Working with these byte literals in DataFrames is quite a hassle, since values such as strings and integers retain their byte format.

Using the client to automatically decode Redis responses will help the user experience, especially when working with the Global State. However, in Python3, the redis-py client's PythonParser throws many errors when working with the decoding. Installing `hiredis` can sidestep these errors.

Example code:
```
import ray
import redis

ray.init()
addr, port = ray.worker.global_worker.redis_address.split(":")
rc = redis.StrictRedis(host=addr, port=port, decode_responses=True)
print(rc.keys())
```
This code will work with hiredis but not without.

issue_title: Version required is wrong, 3.5 features in use in JSONModelRelation
issue_body:
Currently setup.py claims that we're useavle on 3.4+, but I used dict splatting in literals, which hit in 3.5, and didn't catch it bc I use 3.6 to develop.

We should either rewrite that line to use dict.update, or specify 3.5 as min version; given the age of 3.4, I'd be inclined to latter, but I know there's a lot of old Python installs, and Red Hat's antique sensibilities, etc.

We follow a regular scheme for class names, which appends the base class, for example: `SSHChannel`, `IPyParallelExecutor`, etc. For providers, we have a mix: `EC2Provider`, but `Slurm`. I think we should append `Provider` where it is missing for consistency. Parsl users will be using these names for the first time, so I think this should be done as part of #133.

issue_title: Discrete State Space Model Object Support
issue_body:
I'd like to request object support for state space models, especially discrete state space. These are supported in APM MATLAB and APM Python. 
https://github.com/APMonitor/apm_python/blob/master/example_lti_regression/
https://github.com/APMonitor/apm_matlab/tree/master/example_lti

Here is an example model: http://apmonitor.com/wiki/index.php/Apps/LinearStateSpace

The discrete state space model should be able to link with other models. If a discrete state space model is used, a uniform time discretization should be enforced or a warning issued if there are non-uniform time intervals.

There is also the apm_id function in APM MATLAB and APM Python that identifies Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) models from data and writes the APM model. Identification can be a separate / future effort. This request is to add support for state space models.

I suspect you may want to rename this project.  I grant you that the name is clever and I wish you nothing but the best of luck.  But the fact that Dymola is trademarked by a large company with very deep pockets would make me hesitant to give a project, which is very much in a related area, a name that is only one letter difference.

The thing is, the nature of trademark law is such that they have almost no choice but to step in (if they find out about this project) because they can lose their trademark if they don't take steps to defend it.  So if they do [come knocking](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kik_Messenger#Open-source_module_name), just be aware that it isn't (necessarily) that they are trying to be difficult.

issue_title: Reset shared memory agent input to 0 values on exit.
issue_body: When RLBot crashes the agent will continue to use whatever inputs were last used before the crash because they are still filled in shared memory. This causes the agent to usually drive forward in circles and can be unclear that a crash has occurred. If we rest to 0, all agents will stop on crash / termination.

issue_body:
First of all thanks for this awesome effort.

I'm trying to test NMSLib with large 128 dimensional numpy arrays but my code seems to take a long time to index (More than 50 seconds).

Are my parameters unoptimized? I want to aim for more than 10M datapoints but I fear the indexing time grows exponentially.

```
import time

import nmslib
import numpy as np

NUMBER_OF_SAMPLES = 1000000
NUMBER_OF_DIMENSIONS = 128

start_time = time.time()
array = np.random.random_sample((NUMBER_OF_SAMPLES, NUMBER_OF_DIMENSIONS)).astype(np.float32)
print("Finished generating the data => %s seconds" % np.mean(time.time() - start_time))

M = 10
efC = 20
efS = 10
num_nbrs = 5
num_threads = 4
post = 0  # Build the Tree twice but gain accuracy
index_time_params = {'M': M, 'indexThreadQty': num_threads, 'efConstruction': efC, 'post': post}
print('Index-time parameters', index_time_params)

start_time = time.time()
nmslib_index = nmslib.init(method='hnsw', space='cosinesimil')
nmslib_index.addDataPointBatch(array)
nmslib_index.createIndex(index_time_params, print_progress=True)
nmslib_index.setQueryTimeParams({'efSearch': efS})
print("\nTree Initialization Time => %s" % np.mean(time.time() - start_time))
array = None

times = []
for i in range(0, 100):
    start_time = time.time()
    ids, distances = nmslib_index.knnQuery(np.random.rand(1, NUMBER_OF_DIMENSIONS), k=num_nbrs)
    times.append(time.time() - start_time)
print("Average Search Time => %s" % np.mean(times))
```
